subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Sections 8.1–8.3 develop the multiplication and division of fractions with full conceptual grounding (unit-square area model, reciprocal reasoning) and apply them extensively in daily-life contexts: distributing land, internet costs, tea preparation, canal work, oil sharing, brick-covering, and fountain fill-times. Applying fractions in real-life situations is the chapter's primary thread throughout.

Section 8.1 systematically investigates when the product of two fractions is greater than, between, or less than the two factors (E-9), and section 8.2 derives the symmetric relationship between divisor and quotient — both are substantive explorations of fractional number properties and their behaviour, an independent strand from operational application.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The chapter explicitly attributes the multiplication formula to Brahmagupta (628 CE), cancellation to Umasvati (c. 150 CE), and division to Brahmagupta and Bhāskara II (1150 CE). Section 8.3 embeds original problems from Baudhāyana, Pṛithūdakasvāmī, Bhāskarāchārya, and Śhrīdharāchārya, and the 'Pinch of History' traces the full development of fraction theory through Indian mathematics.

The telescoping-product exercises (E-25/E-26) require students to compute partial products, identify the pattern (1−1/2)(1−1/3)…(1−1/n) = 1/n, and prove it by generalisation — a full inductive-to-deductive reasoning cycle producing a general mathematical statement.

The ant-splitting problem (E-25) presents an open-ended chain of fraction multiplications requiring students to discover the telescoping pattern themselves, and the Math Talk on multiplying mixed fractions (5/4 × 3/2) requires inventing a unit-square strategy for a multiplicand greater than 1 — both call for creative problem-solving.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to multiply and divide fractions by developing the conceptual foundations of the operations — unit-square area models, reciprocals, and the relationship between factors and products — and applying them to solve historical and daily-life problems.
